title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: LCFS Market Shows Signs of Strengthening as Second Public Comment Period Nears Closure
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(07th September â€“ 13th October)
  • A total of 211,866 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 56.5% decline week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a fall of 62.2% WoW as 1,051,046 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price increased this week to $66.24/MTâ€¯for Type 1â€¯transfers, recording a 5.61%Â rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 3.63% in prices.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 3.86% WoW; on 15th Oct, it was $68.83/MT. The benchmark price stood at $69.44/MT on 15th Oct.
  • D3 RIN prices continued to decline, dropping 5.20% WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices, each increased by 1.45% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The LCFS market is showing signs of strengthening, as evidenced by the recent uptick in average LCFS spot credit prices and Type 1 weekly average prices. This trend is primarily attributed to anticipation of market tightening, with the closure of the public comment period for CARB's proposed LCFS amendments today. As the public comment period based on CARB's Second 15-Day Notice concludes today, 16th October, 2024, market participants are closely watching for potential impacts of upcoming amendments on credit volumes and prices. The forthcoming hearing on 8th November, 2024, is expected to provide further clarity on the final shape of the LCFS amendments. cCarbon will continue to examine public feedback during this second phase, as was done in the initial phase. CARB is also proposing amendments to the Advanced Clean Fleets (ACF) regulation, focusing on flexibility for fleet exemptions, end-of-life criteria for utility vehicles, and redefining traditional utility specialized vehicles. The amendments incorporate stakeholder feedback. Public webinar is scheduled for 1st November, 2024, to discuss these updates. For a comprehensive overview of the changes and next steps, please read the full article.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_588802" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 5.61% WoW rose from $62.72 to $66.24 this week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices rose by 3.63% compared to the previous week. Type 1 trades saw 211,866 credits exchanged, recording a 56.50% WoW fall this week, but the total transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades had decreased by 62.20% WoW this week, to reach 1,051,046 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_588803" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 3.86% WoW, reaching $68.68/MT. On 15th Oct, the average spot price was $68.83/MT, which significantly rose from $56.58/MT, the lowest point in September. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $69.44/MT as of 15th Oct, up from $57.67/MT as of 10thÂ Sep.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_588804" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 98 trades were reported in the market, with seven high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $100.00 for multiple transactions of Type 2, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $43.85 per credit of Type 2, reported at a volume of 10,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 60,000 credits in Type 2 were undertaken for a price of $57.25, and the smallest volume transfers of 24 credits, a Type 1 category, were undertaken for a price of $64.00/MT.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_588805" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In September, trading volumes rebounded to 3.00 MMT following a sharp decline in August. The recovery gained momentum in the first two weeks of October, with volumes reaching 3.54 MMT, indicating a potential return to July levels soon. During this period, Type 1 prices, which had started to rise in July, continued their upward trend through September and early October, reaching an average spot price of $60.23, reflecting a sustained recovery.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_588806" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 07th Â Â â€“ 13th Oct, total VPT recorded a 16.86% rise (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 5.20% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 1.45% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Oregon Department of Environmental Quality Proposes Rulemaking to Update GHG Measurement under the Clean Fuels Program. The Oregon Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ) proposed rulemaking to bring in modifications to requirements and tools used to measure GHG emissions under the Stateâ€™s Clean Fuels Program. The DEQ has invited public comments on the proposed changes till 21st November 2024 prior to which a public hearing will be held on 19th November 2024. The proposed changes are focused on updates to OR-GREET model to calculate carbon intensity (CI) values, to be in line with the methodology followed by the Californian LCFS program. Further, the proposed rules bring in modifications to requirements for high-risk fuel pathways in addition to bringing increased third party verification and reporting requirements.
  • GFL Environmental and OPAL Fuels Begin Commercial Operations of Their Second RNG Facility. GFL Environmental Inc. (NYSE: GFL) (TSX: GFL) ("GFL") and OPAL Fuels Inc. (Nasdaq: OPAL) announced their joint venture, Paragon RNG has commenced commercial operations of their new renewable natural gas ("RNG") facility at the Sampson County Landfill. The landfill, owned and operated by a subsidiary of GFL, is located in Roseboro, North Carolina. The plant is producing and injecting pipeline quality RNG. EPA registrations have been filed for the project and we expect a normal ramp-up period for the facility.
  • California Department of Transportation to Disburse $206 in Support of Clean Transportation Projects. California Governor Gavin Newsom annouced that the California Department of Transportation would be disbursing $206 towards 149 local clean transportation projects. The funds would be sourced from the California Climate Investment funds in the Low Carbon Transit Operation ProgramÂ  (LCTOP) which in turn receives finances from the cap-and-trade program in the state. While not all projects are directed towards clean transport fleet enhancements, multiple Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV)-related infrastructure investments have been announced.
  • California lawmaker confirms the stateâ€™s Senate will not take up E15 bill. California Assemblywoman Cottie Petrie-Norris on Sept. 24 introduced the E15 bill, ABX2-9. The legislation on Sept. 27 unanimously passed out of the Petroleum and Gasoline Supply Committee. The California Assembly on Oct. 1 voted 68 to zero in favor of the bill.
  • Secretary Naig Announces Record Investments to Expand E15, Biodiesel Availability Statewide. Iowa Secretary of Agriculture Mike Naig announced today that the Iowa Renewable Fuels Infrastructure Program (RFIP) Board recently approved 126 project applications from Iowa gas stations for new and expanded E15, E85, and biodiesel infrastructure projects. Totaling nearly $6.35 million dollars, the projects are located across 61 Iowa counties and will allow drivers to save more money by providing more access to lower cost and cleaner burning homegrown biofuels. The awards were made by the RFIP Board during meetings held in August and September.
  • Oregon DEQ to Update its Advanced Clean Truck Rule and Delay Start to Heavy-Duty Low NOx Omnibus Rule. The Oregon Department of Environmental Quality is bringing temporary rules to the November Environmental Quality Commission meeting to adopt Californiaâ€™s expected minor amendments to its Advanced Clean Trucks regulation. Oregonâ€™s Advanced Clean Truck Rule was adopted in 2021 and is one of the 13 states that has opted in to Californiaâ€™s vehicle and truck emission standards. Changes include adjustments to requirements for truck manufacturers, such as extending the deficit makeup period. Additionally, it makes clear compliance determination and sales reporting rules, which are defined when vehicles are produced and delivered for sale in the state. DEQ is seeking temporary rules to ensure these compliance updates are available for 2025 model year implementation.
